DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-08-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No Appearances.

Cross Defendant The Regents of the University of California's ("The Regents") demurrer to Peet's Coffee, Inc.'s ("Peet's") cross-complaint.

The court adopts the tentative ruling as follows: Cross Defendant The Regents of the University of California's ("The Regents") demurrer to Peet's Coffee, Inc.'s ("Peet's") cross-complaint is overruled. The court rejects The Regents' argument that the parties had no indemnity agreement because it was not "executed prior to the injury." (Labor Code sec. 3864.) Defendant argues that the "Proudly Pouring" Program Agreement, which includes the indemnity provision, was never "executed" and the court cannot rely on the separate signature in the "cover letter" that accompanied that agreement. This argument fails. First, the "X" on the "cover letter" can qualify as a signature. (In re Walker (1895) 110 Cal. 387, 393 [any mark can be sufficient for a signature].) Second, Peet's alleges that the agreement "was fully executed as evidenced by the signature on behalf of UCSF and 'X' at the top of the first page which shows it was executed by [Peet's] through Adobe DocuSign." (Amendment to Cross-Complaint at 4:7-12). The court takes that allegation as true. Third, whether the agreement was executed and the precise terms of the agreement raise disputed factual issues that cannot be resolved on demurrer. The court notes that the pages of the letter/agreement are numbered and the "Proudly Pouring" Program Agreement begins on page three, not page one. That numbering suggests that all of the documents should be read together as a whole. Given the ambiguity, the court cannot rule that the agreement was not executed as a matter of law. The court defers to Peet's characterization of the agreement on demurrer. (Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239 ["While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'"].) The court rejects The Regents argument that Peet's engaged in "active negligence" such that it cannot obtain indemnity. Whether Peet's was negligent (passively or actively) also raises a factual issue. The cross complaint alleges that Peet's engaged in "passive" conduct/negligence at paragraph 25. The court accepts that allegation as true. In addition, Peet's names Dukes Services, Inc. as a cross-defendant and alleges that it is responsible for plaintiff's harm, if any. Liberally reading the cross-complaint per CCP 452, it alleges that the cross-defendants are responsible for the miscalibration of the water.

Judge: Richard B, Ulmer Jr., Clerk: R. Woods, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
